bins.name: Upfront 100% Cost Assignment
bins.desc: Buckeye supports requiring data centers to pay 100% of network upgrade costs upfront, viewing direct assignment as the only approach that reliably prevents stranded cost risk for other ratepayers. A usage-based crediting mechanism can address and-pricing concerns without undermining this protection.
quotes: Large loads, particularly data centers, are driving historic investments in the transmission grid, which have the very real risk of creating significant cost shifts if the large loads do not show up to pay for this investment on a sustained basis.

Direct assignment of upfront costs is the only approach that ensures other ratepayers are not left holding the bag if a large load does not show up on a sustained basis to pay for the investments it has caused.

Rolled-in approaches would force other customers to bear costs they did not cause and expose them to significant stranded cost risk. That is particularly inappropriate given the unique scale, pace, and cost shift risks associated with large data center interconnections.
"and" pricing concerns should not deter the Commission from requiring upfront funding by data centers of the transmission additions they cause.

Crediting would provide the large data center with the opportunity to receive full credits for its upfront payments to the extent that it has sufficient usage over a sustained period of time, thereby protecting other wholesale customers from bearing stranded costs.
The approach of reflecting retail-level solutions in Commission-jurisdictional rates (Part II.C.ii above) should be applied to any large data centers already being studied for interconnection.

bins.name: Federal Wholesale Framework Required
bins.desc: Buckeye argues that retail-only solutions cannot protect other wholesale transmission customers and that a Commission-led federal framework is necessary. An alternative proposal holding the wholesale transmission entity (not the retail data center customer) responsible can address any federal-state jurisdictional concerns.
quotes: A wholesale solution is necessary to ensure that the significant impacts to Commission-jurisdictional transmission rates that data centers are causing do not result in significant cost shifts to other wholesale transmission customers, like Buckeye.

Under this approach, the relevant wholesale transmission entity (not the data center end-user itself) would be held responsible for data center-caused costs at the wholesale level, and that entity could then pass through these costs to the data centers as they and retail regulators see fit at the retail level.
This approach avoids jurisdictional concerns since the Commission will not be imposing a requirement directly on retail data center customers.
Retail-only solutions are insufficient. Buckeye opposes retail-only solutions that fail to address transmission cost shifting to other wholesale customers.

if the Commission does not adopt a wholesale framework consistent with Principle 8 (as described above in Parts II.A and II.B), it must at minimum ensure that retail-level protections against stranded data center transmission costs are reflected at the wholesale level to ensure just, reasonable, and not unduly discriminatory Commission-jurisdictional rates.

bins.name: Usage-Based Crediting Mechanism
bins.desc: Buckeye supports a crediting mechanism tied to actual data center usage rather than automatic balloon payments, ensuring data centers receive credit for upfront costs only as they demonstrate sustained usage, which protects other wholesale customers from bearing stranded costs.
quotes: Crediting would provide the large data center with the opportunity to receive full credits for its upfront payments to the extent that it has sufficient usage over a sustained period of time, thereby protecting other wholesale customers from bearing stranded costs.

bins.name: Inadequacy of Bilateral Agreements
bins.desc: Buckeye opposes relying on individual utility-to-data-center agreements as a substitute for a consistent federal framework, arguing such agreements are insufficiently protective of third-party wholesale customers and create inconsistent treatment across the system.
quotes: The Commission cannot assume that agreements negotiated between one utility and a data center will be sufficiently protective of other customers.

bins.name: Cost Causation Principle
bins.desc: Buckeye consistently invokes cost causation and beneficiary-pays principles to support direct assignment of transmission upgrade costs to data centers. Buckeye opposes rolled-in pricing, nationalized postage stamp rates, and any mechanism that socializes data center costs to other wholesale customers.
quotes: Large loads, particularly data centers, are driving historic investments in the transmission grid, which have the very real risk of creating significant cost shifts if the large loads do not show up to pay for this investment on a sustained basis.

A wholesale solution is necessary to ensure that the significant impacts to Commission-jurisdictional transmission rates that data centers are causing do not result in significant cost shifts to other wholesale transmission customers, like Buckeye.

Direct assignment of upfront costs is the only approach that ensures other ratepayers are not left holding the bag if a large load does not show up on a sustained basis to pay for the investments it has caused.

Rolled-in approaches would force other customers to bear costs they did not cause and expose them to significant stranded cost risk. That is particularly inappropriate given the unique scale, pace, and cost shift risks associated with large data center interconnections.
Buckeye opposes American Electric Power Service Corporation's ("AEP's") proposal to socialize data center transmission costs to other customers on a nationwide basis.
"and" pricing concerns should not deter the Commission from requiring upfront funding by data centers of the transmission additions they cause.

Crediting would provide the large data center with the opportunity to receive full credits for its upfront payments to the extent that it has sufficient usage over a sustained period of time, thereby protecting other wholesale customers from bearing stranded costs.

Under this approach, the relevant wholesale transmission entity (not the data center end-user itself) would be held responsible for data center-caused costs at the wholesale level, and that entity could then pass through these costs to the data centers as they and retail regulators see fit at the retail level.
Retail-only solutions are insufficient. Buckeye opposes retail-only solutions that fail to address transmission cost shifting to other wholesale customers.

if the Commission does not adopt a wholesale framework consistent with Principle 8 (as described above in Parts II.A and II.B), it must at minimum ensure that retail-level protections against stranded data center transmission costs are reflected at the wholesale level to ensure just, reasonable, and not unduly discriminatory Commission-jurisdictional rates.
The Commission cannot assume that agreements negotiated between one utility and a data center will be sufficiently protective of other customers.
The approach of reflecting retail-level solutions in Commission-jurisdictional rates (Part II.C.ii above) should be applied to any large data centers already being studied for interconnection.

bins.name: PJM Cost Socialization Opposition
bins.desc: Buckeye specifically opposes AEP's proposal to spread data center transmission upgrade costs to all customers on a nationwide basis through an extra-high voltage postage stamp rate, noting it merely shifts costs rather than solving the underlying problem.
quotes: Buckeye opposes American Electric Power Service Corporation's ("AEP's") proposal to socialize data center transmission costs to other customers on a nationwide basis.
